stops.name: The Globe (Globe House)
stops.accessNotes: NOT YET AUDITED — do not publish an accessibility claim. The pavement run between the Globe, the Town Hall and Brown's is largely level but the wider loop is NOT step-free. On-site access check required before anything is stated.

beats.name: The butcher's shop — at the Globe
beats.narration: Look up at the front of the Globe and find the old shop doorway. For generations, this building wasn't a bar or a hotel — it was the town butcher's, run by the Gleed family. It mattered enough that when the Globe was listed, the butcher's shop was written into the official record: the listing names it 'Globe House, including S.B. and R.B. Gleed, Butcher's Shop'. Bobby Gleed ran it to the end, and when he retired in 1991 the shop closed — and Laugharne has had no butcher since. For years the shop and the little slaughterhouse behind it were left exactly as they were, untouched. So long before it was anywhere you'd come for dinner, this was where the town came for its meat — a working shop at the centre of a working town.
beats.lookFor: the old shop doorway — a boarded stable door beside a later bay window.

facts.claim: A butcher's shop traded at the Globe, Laugharne, under the Gleed family, closing on Bobby Gleed's retirement in 1991 — since when Laugharne has had no butcher.
sources: Bobby Gleed's Butcher's Shop, Laugharne (photograph, People's Collection Wales item 554337)
Laugharne Lines — 'The Globe' trail stop page

facts.claim: The statutory listing names the property 'Globe House (including SB & RB Gleed, Butcher's Shop)', recording the Gleed butchers' shop as part of the listed building; the shop retains a boarded stable door with a later bay window.
sources: Cadw, Full Report for Listed Buildings — Globe House (including SB & RB Gleed, Butcher's Shop), Laugharne (ref 9664)

facts.claim: In March 2021 the Globe's rear barn was still fitted out as a working abattoir and the Gleeds' butcher's shop was untouched since its last occupation.
sources: Heritage Impact Statement for Conversion Works to Globe House into Retail, Offices and Cafe/Bar
Historic Laugharne abattoir could be given new lease of life as cafe

beats.name: The assembly rooms — at the Globe
beats.narration: Before anyone came to Laugharne for a poet, they came here to dance. The Globe was the town's assembly rooms — where Laugharne gathered for its subscription balls. We can be precise about it: on a Friday in December 1835, the town held its 'Second Laugharne Assembly' right here, with tickets, as the notice put it, 'to be had at the Globe'. Later, when it became the Globe Hotel, that same upstairs ballroom went on serving as the town's assembly rooms. It's worth knowing at the start of a walk, because it tells you what this building has always been for — not one family's house, but the room the whole town used: to meet, to mark an occasion, to be a town together. Quietly, that's the job it's doing again.
beats.lookFor: the tall windows of the upstairs ballroom.

facts.claim: The Globe Inn hosted the Laugharne Assembly subscription balls in the 1830s — the 'Second Laugharne Assembly' was held at the Globe Inn on Fri 18 Dec 1835, tickets 'to be had at the Globe'.
sources: The Welshman, 27 Nov 1835 — 'Second Laugharne Assembly at the Globe Inn' advert

facts.claim: The Globe was the Globe Hotel — a hotel with a gaming room by 1868, sold in 1869, and gone as a hotel by 1912 — its ballroom serving as the town's assembly rooms.
sources: Cadw listed-building record — Globe House, Laugharne (Grade II, ID 9664)
Laugharne Lines — The Globe (local heritage site)

stops.name: The Grist & its Cross
stops.accessNotes: NOT YET AUDITED. Open space in the lower town; believed level, confirm on audit.

beats.name: The Cross — at the Grist
beats.narration: You're at the Grist, near the foot of the town, close to the foreshore — and here stands the Cross. It's a stone Celtic crucifix on a rubble base, with rope carved around it. Look closely and it tells its own small story, because the cross you see isn't the original one. A plaque at the base records that a new cross was set on the old base in 1911, cut by a Laugharne sculptor, G Williams; the same base carries the badge of the Laugharne Corporation. So it's a 1911 cross on a much older footing — the town renewing its own landmark rather than replacing it outright. It's a listed monument now, part of the fabric of the Grist.
beats.lookFor: the rope pattern carved into the stone, the 1911 plaque low on the base, and the Corporation badge stamped beside it.

facts.claim: Cross, The Grist is listed Grade II (Cadw 9630), listed 25 September 1986; Building Class Religious, Ritual and Funerary; sited at the western end of The Grist, close to the foreshore.
sources: Cadw statutory listing 9630 — Cross, The Grist, Laugharne Township

facts.claim: The Cross is a stone Celtic crucifix on a rubble drum base, with rope ornamentation.
sources: Cadw statutory listing 9630 — Cross, The Grist, Laugharne Township

facts.claim: The base plaque reads 'This New Cross was placed on the Old base 1911, G Williams, Sculptor, Laugharne' and is stamped with the badge of Laugharne Corporation.
sources: Cadw statutory listing 9630 — Cross, The Grist, Laugharne Township

stops.name: Brown's Hotel
stops.accessNotes: NOT YET AUDITED. Pavement viewing; a working pub. Check current opening before directing anyone.

beats.name: His favourite pub — Brown's
beats.narration: You're outside Brown's Hotel. Of all the places Dylan Thomas drank in Laugharne, this was the one he loved. It was his favourite pub — he was in here most nights with Caitlin, and he liked it so much he gave out the hotel's telephone number as if it were his own. The official record backs it up: the listing for Brown's notes that Dylan 'frequented the bar during his years in Laugharne', and the inside is hung with his memorabilia now. So this is the real thing — not a place with a loose Dylan rumour, but the pub he genuinely made his own.
beats.lookFor: the memorabilia in the bar, and the windows he'd have sat behind.

facts.claim: The statutory Cadw listing for Brown's Hotel (9638) records that 'Dylan Thomas frequented the bar during his years in Laugharne and the interior is now decorated with memorabilia.'
sources: Cadw statutory listing 9638 — Brown's Hotel, Laugharne Township

facts.claim: Brown's was Dylan Thomas's favourite pub — he drank there most nights with Caitlin and gave the hotel's telephone number as his own.
sources: Wikipedia — Brown's Hotel, Laugharne (signpost, well-cited)
The Dylan Thomas biographies (Ferris; FitzGibbon; D.N. Thomas 'Dylan Remembered'; J.A. Davies)

stops.name: Laugharne Castle
stops.accessNotes: NOT YET AUDITED. View from the accessible green/outer path; interior and keep uneven, separate Cadw opening times. The castle car park floods on high spring tides.

beats.name: Laugharne Castle — the 1644 siege
beats.narration: Face the castle and picture it in the middle of a civil war. In 1644 this wasn't a romantic ruin — it was a stronghold that changed hands twice in a single year. Both the town and the castle were held for the King; then, later in the year, Parliament's forces laid siege to it. The castle surrendered on a Sunday — the third of November, 1644 — and control of Laugharne passed from the King to Parliament. Twice in one year the flag over these walls changed. That siege is part of why the castle stands as a ruin today.
beats.lookFor: the broken walls and towers — the shape of a fortress that was fought over, not just lived in.

facts.claim: Laugharne Castle was besieged by Parliamentarian forces and surrendered on Sunday 3 November 1644.
sources: BCW Project — 'Civil War in South Wales, 1644-5'
Castles, Forts and Battles — 'Laugharne Castle'
Kenyon & Avent (eds), Castles in Wales and the Marches: essays in honour of D. J. Cathcart King

facts.claim: Laugharne Castle changed hands TWICE in 1644 — taken for the King by Gerard in summer, retaken for Parliament in the autumn.
sources: Phillips, 'Memoirs of the Civil War in Wales and the Marches, 1642-1649', vol. II (documents)
Phillips, vol. I (narrative)

facts.claim: In 1644 BOTH the town of Laugharne and the castle were garrisoned for the King.
sources: Phillips, 'Memoirs of the Civil War in Wales and the Marches, 1642-1649', vol. I (narrative)

stops.name: Over Sir John's Hill
stops.accessNotes: NOT YET AUDITED. Reached on a hard, steep, exposed, uneven walk; likely no phone signal — the beat MUST be cached before setting off.

beats.name: The view that earned a poem — Over Sir John's Hill
beats.narration: Look across the water to the long hill on the far side of the bay. That's Sir John's Hill — and Dylan Thomas gave it its own poem, 'Over Sir John's Hill'. He wrote it looking at more or less this view, from the writing shed above the Boathouse: the hill across the bay, the estuary below, the birds working the tide. It's one of those poems where you can stand in the actual place and see the thing the words describe. Give it a minute; this is the view that earned a poem.
beats.lookFor: the long shape of Sir John's Hill across the bay, and the birds over the tide-line.

facts.claim: Dylan Thomas's poem 'Over Sir John's Hill' celebrates the estuary view from his writing shed, Sir John's Hill being the hill across the bay.
sources: Dylan Thomas, 'Over Sir John's Hill' (poem, 1949; pub. In Country Sleep, 1952)
Wikipedia — Dylan Thomas Boathouse
